title: Sts. Agape, Irene & Chiona, St. Pausilypus & St. Herodion of Patras
narrative:
Sts. Agape, Irene, and Chiona the Sister-Martyrs, St. Pausilypus the Martyr, and St. Herodion of Patras

On the eighth of Miazia, the holy Church assembles six witnesses whose martyrdoms form a constellation of related testimonies — the three sisters of Thessalonica, the companion martyred with them, and the apostolic elder who brought the faith to the Greek coastal city where Paul himself had laboured. Saints Agape, Irene, and Chiona were three sisters of Thessalonica martyred under Diocletian, their names meaning Love, Peace, and Snow respectively — and the tradition's preservation of these three names together is itself a theological statement, as though the martyrdom of Love, Peace, and Snow at the same moment and for the same cause were the Church's declaration that these three realities are inseparable, that they rise and fall together, and that the Paschal dawn in whose direction Miazia is moving restores all three simultaneously. The sisters were arrested for possessing the Scriptures during the edict requiring their destruction, and this detail is central to their witness: they died for the written Word, which makes their martyrdom a specific defence of the very texts that the Church reads during the Lenten fast.

The specific form of their deaths is worth dwelling upon: Agape and Chiona were burned, and Irene — who had been discovered with an even larger cache of scriptural texts — was subjected to further indignities before her execution. The tradition records Irene's interrogation in detail, because she answered every question with a directness and theological precision that the court found both admirable and infuriating, citing the Scriptures whose possession had condemned her as the source of her courage. She died having made the court itself into a classroom, the examination into a catechesis, the execution into a liturgy.

Saint Pausilypus is the companion witness of this day — a martyr whose name means the ending of grief, and whose commemorative presence alongside the three sisters in the Miazia dawn has the quality of a promise: that the grief of the martyrs' deaths is not the final word, that the name given him in life was the name that his martyrdom fulfilled, that in the Paschal context toward which Miazia is moving, the ending of grief is precisely what the Resurrection accomplishes. He is the fourth note of the chord that Agape, Irene, and Chiona sound, and together they produce the harmony of the complete Paschal testimony.

Saint Herodion of Patras is among the most historically situated of the New Testament saints: a kinsman of the Apostle Paul, greeted by him in the Letter to the Romans alongside Andronicus and Junia, who became by tradition the bishop of the Achaian city of Patras — the same city where the Apostle Andrew had laboured and been crucified on his X-shaped cross. Herodion thus stands in a line of apostolic succession that connects Paul through him to the martyrdom of Andrew, and his commemoration in the dawn of Miazia places the ancient apostolic mission to Greece at the threshold of the Paschal feast that the apostles had died to make possible. Amen.
